title: Matsvinskollen
subtitle: Designing a quiz-based interaction system using reusable components and feedback states.

intro.heading: Structuring an interactive learning concept
intro.body:
Matsvinskollen was a three week university project exploring how a quiz-based experience could help people learn practical knowledge about food waste.

Instead of building a complete product, the goal was to prototype a simple interaction that could communicate knowledge through short questions and immediate feedback.

role.heading: My contribution
role.body:
My main contribution focused on structuring the interaction and building the prototype in Figma. I designed the quiz interaction, created the visual layout for the mobile interface, and built a reusable component system that allowed the prototype to scale as we added more questions.

I also structured the prototype flow so that feedback, navigation, and interaction states behaved consistently throughout the experience.

designChallenge.heading: Design challenge
designChallenge.body: As the quiz grew, the interface needed a clear structure so multiple screens and interactions could be built without becoming inconsistent. Instead of designing each screen separately, the challenge became creating a system where interactions, feedback states, and UI elements could be reused across the prototype.

componentSystem.heading: Designing reusable interaction states
componentSystem.body:
To keep the interface consistent, I built the quiz interactions using reusable components and Figma properties.

Each answer option uses interaction states such as default, hover, correct answer, and incorrect answer. This allowed the prototype to clearly communicate feedback while keeping the interaction logic consistent across the entire quiz.

image.alt: Reusable interaction states for quiz answers

systemStructure.heading: Structuring the component system
systemStructure.body:
The interface was organised using reusable components such as answer buttons, progress indicators, and feedback icons. By structuring the design system this way, new quiz screens could be built quickly while maintaining visual and interaction consistency.

This approach also made the prototype easier to iterate on as the project evolved.

prototypeFlow.heading: Building the quiz flow
prototypeFlow.body:
I structured the full quiz flow inside Figma so that each question could trigger feedback before moving the user forward.

Correct answers move the user to the next question, while incorrect answers trigger an overlay explaining the result. This structure allowed the prototype to simulate a learning loop where feedback immediately reinforces the knowledge being tested.

outcome.heading: Outcome
outcome.body: The prototype demonstrated how a simple quiz interaction can turn abstract knowledge about food waste into short learning moments. By combining clear feedback states with a structured interaction flow, the concept made it easier to communicate practical information in an engaging way.

keyLearning.heading: Key learning
keyLearning.body: Working with a component-based approach made the prototype easier to scale and iterate on. Structuring interactions through reusable states helped maintain clarity across the experience while allowing the design to evolve quickly during the short project timeframe.
